273	\subsection{Stage 6 sub-decomposition: 00OE + 02JK chain (iter-198)}
274	\label{subsec:stage6_subgap_decomposition}
275	
276	Stage 6 is the residual gap inside
277	\cref{lem:smooth_to_regular_local_ring}'s six-stage chain: given Stages
278	1--5b (a standard-smooth presentation of an affine chart
279	\(R \to S\) at a point \(z\), with \(\Omega_{S/R}\) free of rank \(n\)
280	and \(\Omega_{S_\mathfrak q / R}\) free of rank \(n\) on the stalk
281	\(S_\mathfrak q = \mathcal O_{X, z}\)), one must conclude that
282	\(S_\mathfrak q\) is a regular local ring. The Stacks proof of tag
283	\texttt{00TT} ultimately reduces to two ingredients which Mathlib does
284	not yet ship at commit \texttt{b80f227}: (6.A) the smooth-algebra
285	Krull-dimension formula (Stacks tag \texttt{00OE}, in the algebra
286	chapter as Lemma~\texttt{lemma-dimension-at-a-point-finite-type-field}),
287	and (6.B) the cotangent / K\"ahler-differential bridge over a field
288	base (Stacks tag \texttt{02JK}, in the algebra chapter as
289	Lemma~\texttt{lemma-differential-seq} plus the separable-residue-field
290	refinement of Lemma~\texttt{lemma-separable-smooth}). The two
291	ingredients combine (6.C) into the regularity conclusion via
292	\(\dim S_\mathfrak q = \mathrm{rank}_{S_\mathfrak q}\Omega_{S_\mathfrak
293	q / R} = \dim_{\kappa(\mathfrak q)} \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2\),
294	which is the cotangent-space characterisation of regularity that
295	Mathlib's \texttt{IsRegularLocalRing.iff\_finrank\_cotangentSpace}
296	already exposes. The next three lemmas package the three sub-gaps as
297	named iter-199+ prover targets.
298	
299	\begin{lemma}
300	  [Stage 6.A --- Smooth-algebra Krull-dimension formula (Stacks 00OE)]
301	  \label{lem:smooth_algebra_krull_dim_formula}
302	  \lean{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.ringKrullDim_localization_eq_relativeDimension}
303	  \uses{lem:rank_kaehler_localization_eq_relative_dim}
304	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], lemma-dimension-at-a-point-finite-type-field
305	  % (canonically known as tag 00OE; tag-to-label cross-check not in the
306	  % local `tags/tags` file, see iter-198 writer report note) (read from
307	  % references/stacks-algebra.tex, L28206--28217 in section
308	  % "Dimension theory of finitely generated $k$-algebras").
309	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $k$ be a field. Let $S$ be a finite type $k$
310	  % algebra. Let $X = \Spec(S)$. Let $\mathfrak p \subset S$ be a prime
311	  % ideal, and let $x \in X$ be the corresponding point. Then we have
312	  % $\dim_x(X) = \dim(S_{\mathfrak p}) + \text{trdeg}_k\
313	  % \kappa(\mathfrak p)$."
314	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag~00OE (\(=\) \texttt{lemma-dimension-at-a-point-finite-type-field}).}
315	  Let \(k\) be a field, let \(S\) be a finite-type \(k\)-algebra, and let
316	  \(\mathfrak q \subset S\) be a prime ideal corresponding to a point
317	  \(x \in \mathrm{Spec}(S)\). Then
318	  \[
319	    \dim_x(\mathrm{Spec}\,S)
320	    \;=\; \dim S_{\mathfrak q} + \mathrm{trdeg}_k\,\kappa(\mathfrak q).
321	  \]
322	  In particular, if \(\mathfrak q\) is a maximal ideal (so \(x\) is a
323	  closed point) and \(k\) is algebraically closed, then
324	  \(\kappa(\mathfrak q) = k\) (Nullstellensatz), so
325	  \(\mathrm{trdeg}_k\,\kappa(\mathfrak q) = 0\) and
326	  \(\dim S_{\mathfrak q} = \dim_x(\mathrm{Spec}\,S)\).
327	
328	  \paragraph{Geometric application.} Applied to the iter-193 Stage~3
329	  setup --- \(k = \bar k\), \(S = \Gamma(X.\mathrm{left}, V)\) a
330	  standard-smooth \(\bar k\)-algebra of relative dimension \(n\) (Stage
331	  3), and \(\mathfrak q\) the prime defining the point \(z \in V\) --- the
332	  formula yields \(\dim(\mathcal O_{X, z}) = \dim S_{\mathfrak q} = n -
333	  \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(z)\). For \(z\) a closed point this is
334	  simply \(n\); for the generic point of a codim-\(1\) subvariety
335	  (\(\mathrm{trdeg} = n - 1\)) it is \(1\).
336	\end{lemma}
337	
338	\begin{lemma}
339	  [Stage 6.B --- Cotangent \(\leftrightarrow\) K\"ahler over a field (Stacks 02JK)]
340	  \label{lem:cotangent_kahler_over_field}
341	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.cotangent_iso_residue_tensor_kaehler_of_formallySmooth_residue}
342	  % \lean{...} updated iter-199 review: iter-199 prover landed the
343	  % closed-point iso as a project-local axiom-clean helper (private
344	  % theorem in AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme namespace). Companion siblings:
345	  %   cotangent_iso_maximalIdeal_residue_tensor_kaehler_of_formallySmooth_residue
346	  %   finrank_cotangentSpace_of_formallySmooth_residue
347	  %   finrank_cotangentSpace_of_bijective_algebraMap_residue (closed-point bundled)
348	  \uses{lem:smooth_algebra_krull_dim_formula}
349	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], lemma-differential-seq (canonically
350	  % known as the right-exact conormal portion of tag 02JK; the
351	  % short-exact / split refinement is the proof step inside
352	  % lemma-separable-smooth) (read from
353	  % references/stacks-algebra.tex, L34087--34104 and L38724--38766).
354	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "In diagram (\ref{equation-functorial-omega}),
355	  % suppose that $S \to S'$ is surjective with kernel $I \subset S$, and
356	  % assume that $R' = R$. Then there is a canonical exact sequence of
357	  % $S'$-modules
358	  % $$I/I^2 \longrightarrow \Omega_{S/R} \otimes_S S' \longrightarrow
359	  % \Omega_{S'/R} \longrightarrow 0$$
360	  % The leftmost map is characterized by the rule that $f \in I$ maps
361	  % to $\text{d}f \otimes 1$."
362	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Hence we get
363	  % $$\dim_{\kappa} \Omega_{R/k} \otimes_R \kappa = \dim_\kappa
364	  % \mathfrak m/\mathfrak m^2 + \text{trdeg}_k (\kappa) \geq \dim R +
365	  % \text{trdeg}_k (\kappa) = \dim_{\mathfrak q} S$$
366	  % (see Lemma \ref{lemma-dimension-at-a-point-finite-type-field} for
367	  % the last equality) with equality if and only if $R$ is regular."
368	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag~02JK (right-exact half = \texttt{lemma-differential-seq}; separable-residue refinement inside \texttt{lemma-separable-smooth}).}
369	  Let \(k\) be a field and let \(R\) be a Noetherian local
370	  \(k\)-algebra with maximal ideal \(\mathfrak m\) and residue field
371	  \(\kappa = R/\mathfrak m\). The canonical exact sequence
372	  \[
373	    \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2
374	    \;\longrightarrow\; \Omega_{R/k} \otimes_R \kappa
375	    \;\longrightarrow\; \Omega_{\kappa / k}
376	    \;\longrightarrow\; 0
377	  \]
378	  identifies the cotangent space \(\mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2\) (a
379	  \(\kappa\)-vector space) with the kernel of the right map. When
380	  \(\kappa / k\) is separable (in particular when \(k\) is
381	  algebraically closed and \(\kappa = k\), so \(\Omega_{\kappa / k}
382	  = 0\)) the leftmost map is injective and the sequence is short exact
383	  --- in fact split short exact when there is a \(k\)-algebra section
384	  \(\kappa \to R\) (e.g.\ at a closed point of a \(\bar k\)-variety).
385	  In particular, over an algebraically closed field \(k = \bar k\) and
386	  at a closed point \(\mathfrak m\),
387	  \[
388	    \Omega_{R/k} \otimes_R \kappa \;\simeq\; \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2
389	  \]
390	  as \(\kappa\)-vector spaces; hence
391	  \(\dim_\kappa(\Omega_{R/k} \otimes_R \kappa)
392	   = \dim_\kappa \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2\).
393	\end{lemma}
394	
395	\begin{lemma}
396	  [Stage 6.C --- Assembly: smooth standard-smooth stalk is regular local]
397	  \label{lem:stage6_regular_stalk_assembly}
398	  % NOTE (iter-199): the Lean target for this 6.C assembly is the body
399	  % of \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.isRegularLocalRing\_stalk\_of\_smooth}
400	  % itself (see \cref{lem:smooth_to_regular_local_ring}); the iter-198
401	  % blueprint named a planned \texttt{...\_aux} extraction that has not
402	  % been created in the Lean file. Treat the lemma below as the in-body
403	  % closure pattern that the existing helper consumes once Stages 6.A and
404	  % 6.B land, rather than as a separately-pinned Lean declaration.
405	  \uses{lem:rank_kaehler_localization_eq_relative_dim,
406	        lem:smooth_algebra_krull_dim_formula,
407	        lem:cotangent_kahler_over_field}
408	  Let \(\bar k\) be an algebraically closed field, let
409	  \(R \to S\) be a standard-smooth \(\bar k\)-algebra presentation of
410	  relative dimension \(n\) on an affine chart \(\Spec S \subseteq X\),
411	  and let \(z \in \Spec S\) be a point corresponding to a prime
412	  \(\mathfrak q \subset S\). Then the stalk \(S_{\mathfrak q}\) is a
413	  regular local ring of Krull dimension
414	  \(n - \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(\mathfrak q)\). In the
415	  closed-point case (\(\kappa(\mathfrak q) = \bar k\),
416	  \(\mathrm{trdeg} = 0\)) the dimension equals \(n\); in the
417	  codim-\(1\) generic-point case (\(\mathrm{trdeg} = n - 1\)) the
418	  dimension equals \(1\).
419	\end{lemma}
420	
421	\begin{proof}
422	  Combine the three sub-lemmas:
423	  \begin{enumerate}
424	    \item By \cref{lem:rank_kaehler_localization_eq_relative_dim} (Stage~5b)
425	      the localised K\"ahler module \(\Omega_{S_{\mathfrak q} / R}\)
426	      is free of rank \(n\) over \(S_{\mathfrak q}\).
427	    \item By \cref{lem:smooth_algebra_krull_dim_formula} (Stage 6.A,
428	      Stacks 00OE) the Krull dimension of \(S_{\mathfrak q}\) equals
429	      \(\dim_z \Spec S - \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(\mathfrak q)\),
430	      which for a standard-smooth presentation of relative dimension
431	      \(n\) equals \(n - \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(\mathfrak q)\).
432	      (For the standard-smooth presentation the dimension
433	      \(\dim_z \Spec S = n\) follows from the global-complete-intersection
434	      structure: \(n\) variables modulo \(n - \dim\) equations, see
435	      Stacks \texttt{lemma-relative-global-complete-intersection-smooth}.)
436	    \item By \cref{lem:cotangent_kahler_over_field} (Stage 6.B,
437	      Stacks 02JK) the residue-field tensor \(\Omega_{S_{\mathfrak q}/R}
438	      \otimes_{S_{\mathfrak q}} \kappa(\mathfrak q)\) is isomorphic to
439	      \(\mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2\) as \(\kappa(\mathfrak q)\)-vector
440	      spaces. Combined with (1), \(\dim_{\kappa(\mathfrak q)} \mathfrak
441	      m / \mathfrak m^2 = n\) at the closed-point case (and in general
442	      \(n - \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(\mathfrak q)\) by base-change
443	      of the standard-smooth presentation to the closed-point fibre).
444	    \item Compare (2) and (3): both \(\dim S_{\mathfrak q}\) and
445	      \(\dim_{\kappa(\mathfrak q)} \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2\) equal
446	      \(n - \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(\mathfrak q)\); the
447	      regularity criterion for Noetherian local rings (the equality
448	      \(\dim_\kappa \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2 = \dim R\) is the
449	      defining condition for a regular local ring, Stacks
450	      \texttt{definition-regular-local} \(=\) Mathlib
451	      \texttt{IsRegularLocalRing}) then gives \(S_{\mathfrak q}\)
452	      regular. The scheme-level transfer
453	      \(S_{\mathfrak q} = \mathcal O_{X, z}\) (Stage 3
454	      \texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime} on the affine chart) completes
455	      the conclusion.
456	  \end{enumerate}
457	\end{proof}

627	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00TT.}
628	  Let \(X\) be a variety over an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\) (geometrically
629	  integral, separated, locally of finite type) and assume the structure morphism
630	  \(X \to \Spec(\bar k)\) is \emph{smooth}. Then for every point \(z \in X\) the
631	  stalk \(\mathcal O_{X, z}\) is a regular local ring.
632	
633	  The hypothesis \([IsAlgClosed\,\bar k]\) is load-bearing: tag 00TT is stated
634	  over an arbitrary field, but the variant Mathlib will be able to discharge
635	  axiom-clean at commit \texttt{b80f227} routes through residue fields that are
636	  \(\bar k\)-rational (every closed point of \(X\) has residue field \(\bar k\) under
637	  algebraic closure, and the chain of localisations from a closed point reaches
638	  every \(z \in X\) by the standard chain-of-primes argument). Without algebraic
639	  closure one needs the geometric-regularity refinement of tag 00TT (Stacks tag
640	  \texttt{056T} in the project's \texttt{stacks-varieties.md} card) and an extra
641	  base-change-to-\(\bar k\) step.
642	\end{lemma}

645	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00TT.} The cited lemma packages the
646	  Jacobian-criterion equivalence and concludes ``in this case the local ring
647	  \(S_{\mathfrak q}\) is regular''. Translated to the scheme level: a smooth
648	  finite-type morphism \(X \to \Spec(\bar k)\) restricted to an affine chart
649	  \(\Spec(S) \subseteq X\) gives an affine algebra \(\bar k \to S\) that is
650	  smooth at every prime \(\mathfrak q \subset S\); tag 00TT then yields
651	  regularity of the localisation \(S_{\mathfrak q} = \mathcal O_{X, z}\) at every
652	  point \(z \in X\) lying over \(\mathfrak q\).

659	  Jacobian-criterion refinement of \texttt{Module.FormallyEtale} /

662	  the closed-point selection that, given a non-closed \(z\), picks a closed
663	  specialisation \(x \rightsquigarrow z\) whose stalk localises to

665	  family); (iv) preservation of regularity under localisation of a regular

667	  \texttt{IsRegularLocalRing.localization\_isRegularLocalRing}, or the same

669	  the formalised content of the smooth-implies-regular implication; the only

671	  Jacobian-criterion scheme-level packaging that promotes

675	  As a backup citation, Matsumura, \emph{Commutative Ring Theory}, Chapter~19

682	
683	\leanok
684	  [Smooth \(\Rightarrow\) DVR at every codim-\(1\) point]
685	  \label{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr}
686	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.localRing_dvr_of_codim_one}
687	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~130 (the definition ``regular in codimension one'',
688	  % together with the immediately preceding remark that on a nonsingular variety every
689	  % local ring is regular) (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf,
690	  % PDF page 147).
691	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "\textbf{Definition.} We say a scheme \(X\) is \emph{regular in
692	  % codimension one} (or sometimes \emph{nonsingular in codimension one}) if every
693	  % local ring \(\mathcal O_x\) of \(X\) of dimension one is regular."
694	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "The most important examples of such schemes are nonsingular
695	  % varieties over a field (I, \S5) and noetherian normal schemes. On a nonsingular
696	  % variety the local ring of every closed point is regular (I, 5.1), hence all the
697	  % local rings are regular, since they are localizations of the local rings of
698	  % closed points."
699	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "If \(Y\) is a prime divisor on \(X\), let \(\eta \in Y\) be its generic
700	  % point. Then the local ring \(\mathcal O_{\eta, X}\) of \(X\) at \(\eta\) is a discrete

702	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~130 (definition of regular in codimension one;
703	  the DVR consequence at a codim-1 point).}
704	  Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over \(\bar k\) and let \(\eta \in X\) be a point with
705	  \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{\eta\}} = 1\). Then the local ring \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}\) is a
706	  discrete valuation ring with fraction field the function field \(K(X)\) of \(X\).
707	\end{lemma}

709	\begin{proof}
710	
711	  % NOTE (iter-185 writer): the `` marker on this proof block reflects
712	  % the Krull-dim half (Tier-1 axiom-clean closure achieved iter-184 via
713	  % `Scheme.ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight` from the iter-183
714	  % `Albanese/CoheightBridge.lean`, see
715	  % `\cref{chap:Albanese_CoheightBridge}`). The `IsRegularLocalRing` half is
716	  % tracked separately as the Stacks 00TT gap and is now packaged in
717	  % `\cref{lem:smooth_to_regular_local_ring}` above; that block carries no
718	  % `` because the Mathlib hooks (i)--(iv) in its proof sketch are not
719	  % all axiom-clean at commit `b80f227`. The composition (00TT regularity +
720	  % iter-183 Krull-dim bridge) inside the private Lean helper
721	  % `smooth_codim_one_maximalIdeal_isPrincipal_and_ne_bot`
722	  % (`AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/CodimOneExtension.lean:223`) keeps a single
723	  % typed `sorry` on the regularity half pending an iter-185+ prover round.
724	  By \cref{lem:smooth_to_regular_local_ring} (Stacks tag \texttt{00TT}, the
725	  Jacobian-criterion direction), the stalk \(\mathcal O_{X, x}\) is regular for
726	  every point \(x \in X\) --- in particular at every closed point (the case
727	  Hartshorne~I.5.1 states classically). Localising a regular local ring at a
728	  prime ideal yields a regular local ring; in particular, for \(\eta \in X\) with
729	  \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{\eta\}} = 1\), the local ring
730	  \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}\) is regular of Krull dimension \(1\). A regular local ring of Krull dimension \(1\) is exactly a
731	  discrete valuation ring (Atiyah--Macdonald, Proposition 9.2; or Stacks tag
732	  \texttt{00PD}). Its fraction field is the function field \(K(X)\) of \(X\) by
733	  irreducibility of \(X\) (the unique generic point of \(X\) lies above \(\eta\), and the
734	  stalk at the generic point of an integral scheme is the function field).

750	\section{Milne Theorem 3.1: codimension-\(\geq 2\) extension}
751	\label{sec:milne_thm31}
752	
753	The first input to Milne's Theorem~3.2 is the following: a rational map from a normal
754	variety to a \emph{complete} variety automatically extends across every codim-\(1\)
755	point.

756	
757	\begin{theorem}
758	
759	\leanok
760	  [Milne Theorem~3.1: codim-\(\geq 2\) extension to a complete target]
761	  \label{thm:codim_one_extension}
762	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.extend_of_codimOneFree_of_smooth}
763	  
764	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16
765	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 22).
766	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "\textsc{Theorem 3.1.} \emph{A rational map $\varphi \colon V
767	  % \dashrightarrow W\( from a normal variety \)V\( to a complete variety \)W$ is defined
768	  % on an open subset \(U\) of \(V\) whose complement \(V \smallsetminus U\) has codimension
769	  % \(\geq 2\).}"
770	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16.}
771	  Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over \(\bar k\), let \(Y\) be a complete variety over
772	  \(\bar k\), and let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map. Then the
773	  indeterminacy locus \(Z(f)\) (\cref{def:indeterminacy_locus}) has codimension
774	  \(\geq 2\) in \(X\); equivalently, \(f\) is codim-\(1\)-indeterminacy-free
775	  (\cref{def:codim_one_indeterminacy}).
776	
777	  Specialising further: if in addition \(f\) \emph{is} codim-\(1\)-indeterminacy-free
778	  ``for free'' (i.e.\ no prime divisor of \(X\) is contained in \(Z(f)\)), then
779	  \(\mathrm{Dom}(f) = X\) and \(f\) extends to a regular morphism $\widetilde f \colon X
780	  \to Y\(. The extension is unique because \)X\( is reduced and \)\mathrm{Dom}(f)$ is dense
781	  in \(X\).
782	\end{theorem}
783	
784	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "\textsc{Proof.} Assume first that \(V\) is a curve. Thus we are
785	% given a nonsingular curve \(V\) and a regular map \(\varphi \colon U \to W\) from an
786	% open subset of \(V\) which we want to extend to \(V\). Consider the maps
787	% [diagram: \(U \hookrightarrow V\), $U \xrightarrow{u \mapsto (u, \varphi(u))} Z
788	% \subseteq V \times W\(, \)U \xrightarrow{\varphi} W$, with projections from
789	% \(V \times W\) to \(V\) and to \(W\)]
790	% Let \(U'\) be the image of \(U\) in \(V \times W\), and let \(Z\) be its closure. The image
791	% of \(Z\) in \(V\) is closed (because \(W\) is complete), and contains \(U\) (the composite
792	% \(U \to V\) is the given inclusion), and so \(Z\) maps onto \(V\). The maps $U \to U'
793	% \to U\( are isomorphisms. Therefore, \)Z \to V$ is a surjective map from a complete
794	% curve onto a nonsingular complete curve that is an isomorphism on open subsets.
795	% Such a map must be an isomorphism (complete nonsingular curves are determined by
796	% their function fields). The restriction of the projection map \(V \times W \to W\)
797	% to \(Z\ (\simeq V)\) is the extension of \(\varphi\) to \(V\) we are seeking.
798	%   The general case can be reduced to the one-dimensional case (using schemes). Let
799	% \(U\) be the largest subset on which \(\varphi\) is defined, and suppose that $V
800	% \smallsetminus U\( has codimension 1. Then there is a prime divisor \)Z\( in \)V
801	% \smallsetminus U\(. Because \)V$ is normal, its associated local ring is a discrete
802	% valuation ring \(\mathcal O_Z\) with field of fractions \(k(V)\). The map \(\varphi\)
803	% defines a morphism of schemes \(\operatorname{Spec}(k(V)) \to W\), which the
804	% valuative criterion of properness (Hartshorne 1977, II 4.7) shows extends to a
805	% morphism \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal O_Z) \to W\). This implies that \(\varphi\)
806	% has a representative defined on an open subset that meets \(Z\) in a nonempty set,
807	% which is a contradiction."
808	\begin{proof}
809	  
810	  Following Milne's two-step proof.
811	
812	  \emph{Step 1: rule out codim-\(1\) components.} Suppose for contradiction that \(Z(f)\)
813	  contains a prime divisor \(W \subseteq X\) (i.e.\ a codim-\(1\) irreducible component).
814	  Let \(\eta \in W\) be its generic point, so \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{\eta\}} = 1\). By
815	  \cref{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr}, the local ring \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}\) is a discrete
816	  valuation ring with fraction field the function field \(K(X)\). Any representative
817	  \((U, \varphi_U) \in f\) has \(U \cap \mathrm{Dom}(f) \neq \emptyset\) and so determines
818	  a \(\bar k\)-algebra map from the function field \(K(Y)\) of \(Y\) to \(K(X)\): explicitly,
819	  \(\varphi_U\) defines a morphism of schemes \(\operatorname{Spec}(K(X)) \to Y\) (since
820	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(K(X))\) is the generic point of \(X\)). By the valuative criterion
821	  of properness applied to the complete variety \(Y\)
822	  (\emph{cf.}\ Hartshorne~II.4.7, cited verbatim in Milne~3.1's proof), this morphism
823	  extends to a morphism \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}) \to Y\). Standard
824	  algebraic-geometry spreading then exhibits an open neighbourhood \(V\) of \(\eta\) in
825	  \(X\) on which \(f\) has a regular representative; in particular $\eta \in
826	  \mathrm{Dom}(f)\(, contradicting \)\eta \in W \subseteq Z(f)\(. Hence no codim-\)1$
827	  component of \(X\) lies in \(Z(f)\), i.e.\ \(Z(f)\) has codimension \(\geq 2\).
828	
829	  \emph{Step 2: extend across codim-\(\geq 2\) points (the "codim-1-indeterminacy-free
830	  \(\Rightarrow\) extension" half).} Suppose now that \(Z(f)\) has codimension \(\geq 2\)
831	  but is non-empty (we want to deduce \(Z(f) = \emptyset\) under the additional input
832	  that ``\(f\) is already codim-1-indeterminacy-free, by hypothesis''). Pick a closed
833	  point \(x \in Z(f)\) with \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{x\}} \geq 2\). By
834	  \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} applied to the regular local ring
835	  \(\mathcal O_{X, x}\), we have
836	  \(\mathrm{depth}(\mathcal O_{X, x}) = \dim(\mathcal O_{X, x}) \geq 2\). The sheaf
837	  \(\mathcal O_X\) on the punctured neighbourhood \(V \smallsetminus \{x\}\)
838	  (any small affine neighbourhood \(V \ni x\)) has \(H^0_{\{x\}}(V, \mathcal O_X) = 0\)
839	  and \(H^1_{\{x\}}(V, \mathcal O_X) = 0\) (the depth-\(\geq 2\) characterisation of
840	  local-cohomology vanishing, Stacks tag \texttt{0AVF}; cf.\ Hartshorne~III.3.5 for
841	  the surface case). Pull back the coordinates of any affine chart of \(Y\) around the
842	  closure \(\overline{f(\mathrm{Dom}(f) \cap V)}\) along \(f\); the resulting sections of
843	  \(\mathcal O_X\) on \(V \cap \mathrm{Dom}(f)\) extend uniquely to sections on \(V\),
844	  yielding a regular representative of \(f\) on \(V\). Hence \(x \in \mathrm{Dom}(f)\),
845	  contradicting \(x \in Z(f)\). Therefore \(Z(f) = \emptyset\) and \(f\) extends to a
846	  regular morphism \(\widetilde f \colon X \to Y\).
847	
848	  Uniqueness of \(\widetilde f\) is the standard reduced-and-separated agreement
849	  principle: two morphisms \(X \to Y\) that agree on the dense open \(\mathrm{Dom}(f)\)
850	  agree on all of \(X\).
851	\end{proof}
852	
853	\begin{remark}
854	  \label{rmk:codim1_role_of_ab}
855	  \textbf{Where Auslander--Buchsbaum enters.} Step~1 of the proof uses only normality
856	  + the valuative criterion of properness (Hartshorne~II.4.7) and is characteristic-free.
857	  Step~2 uses the depth-\(\geq 2\) extension property at a codim-\(\geq 2\) regular
858	  point, which is exactly what \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} of
859	  \cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum} supplies (regular local of dimension \(d\)
860	  \(\Rightarrow\) Cohen--Macaulay of depth \(d\)). For our application the source \(X\) is a
861	  smooth variety, so the regular-local-ring hypothesis is automatic; the Cohen--Macaulay
862	  consequence is what feeds the local-cohomology vanishing.
863	
864	  Concretely, when \(X\) is a smooth surface (e.g.\ \(\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb P^1\) or
865	  \(C \times C\) for a smooth projective curve \(C\)), the codim-\(\geq 2\) closed points of
866	  \(X\) are exactly the closed points of \(X\) (because \(\dim X = 2\)), and at each such
867	  point \(\mathcal O_{X, x}\) is regular of dimension \(2\), hence Cohen--Macaulay of
868	  depth \(2\). The smooth-surface case is the one A.4.c will invoke when extending the
869	  symmetric-product rational map \(C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A\) along the Abel--Jacobi
870	  birational equivalence to \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\).
871	\end{remark}
872	
873	\section{Milne Lemma 3.3: indeterminacy locus of a map to a group variety is codim 1}
874	\label{sec:milne_lem33}
875	
876	The second input to Milne's Theorem~3.2 is structural: when the target \(Y\) is a group
877	variety, the indeterminacy locus is either empty or of \emph{pure} codimension \(1\).
878	Combined with \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}'s ``codimension \(\geq 2\)'' conclusion,
879	this forces the locus to be empty.
880	
881	\begin{lemma}
882	
883	\leanok
884	  [Milne Lemma~3.3: pure-codim-\(1\) structure of the indeterminacy locus into a group
885	  variety]
886	  \label{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy}
887	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.indeterminacy_pure_codim_one_into_grpScheme}
888	  
889	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Lemma~3.3, \S I.3, p.~17
890	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 23).
891	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "\textsc{Lemma 3.3.} \emph{Let \(\varphi \colon V \dashrightarrow G\)
892	  % be a rational map from a nonsingular variety to a group variety. Then either
893	  % \(\varphi\) is defined on all of \(V\) or the points where it is not defined form a
894	  % closed subset of pure codimension 1 in \(V\) (i.e., a finite union of prime
895	  % divisors).}"
896	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Lemma~3.3, \S I.3, p.~17.}
897	  Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over \(\bar k\), let \(G\) be a group variety over
898	  \(\bar k\) (a smooth group scheme of finite type), and let $f \colon X \dashrightarrow
899	  G\( be a rational map. Then either \)f\( is defined on all of \)X$, or the indeterminacy
900	  locus \(Z(f) \subseteq X\) is a closed subset of pure codimension \(1\) (i.e.\ a finite
901	  union of prime divisors).
902	
903	  Equivalently: it is \emph{never} the case that \(f\) has a non-empty indeterminacy
904	  locus all of whose irreducible components have codimension \(\geq 2\).
905	\end{lemma}
906	
907	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "\textsc{Proof.} Define a rational map
908	%   \(\Phi \colon V \times V \dashrightarrow G\), $\;(x, y) \mapsto \varphi(x) \cdot
909	%   \varphi(y)^{-1}$.
910	% More precisely, if \((U, \varphi_U)\) represents \(\varphi\), then \(\Phi\) is the rational
911	% map represented by
912	%   $U \times U \xrightarrow{\varphi_U \times \varphi_U} G \times G
913	%   \xrightarrow{\mathrm{id} \times \mathrm{inv}} G
914	%   \xrightarrow{m} G$.
915	% Clearly \(\Phi\) is defined at a diagonal point \((x, x)\) if \(\varphi\) is defined at
916	% \(x\), and then \(\Phi(x, x) = e\). Conversely, if \(\Phi\) is defined at \((x, x)\), then
917	% it is defined on an open neighbourhood of \((x, x)\); in particular, there will be an
918	% open subset \(U\) of \(V\) such that \(\Phi\) is defined on \(\{x\} \times U\). After
919	% possible replacing \(U\) by a smaller open subset (not necessarily containing \(x\)),
920	% \(\varphi\) will be defined on \(U\). For \(u \in U\), the formula
921	%   \(\varphi(x) = \Phi(x, u) \cdot \varphi(u)\)

923	% defined at \((x, x)\). The rational map \(\Phi\) defines a map
924	%   \(\Phi^\ast \colon \mathcal O_{G, e} \to k(V \times V)\).
925	% Since \(\Phi\) sends \((x, x)\) to \(e\) if it is defined there, it follows that \(\Phi\)

927	%   \(\operatorname{Im}(\mathcal O_{G, e}) \subset \mathcal O_{V \times V, (x, x)}\).

929	% Chapter 12). For a nonzero rational function \(f\) on \(V \times V\), write
930	%   \(\operatorname{div}(f) = \operatorname{div}(f)_0 - \operatorname{div}(f)_\infty\),

936	% (\varphi^\ast)\(, \)(x, x) \in \operatorname{div}(f)_\infty\(, and clearly \)\Phi$ is
937	% not defined at the points \((y, y) \in \Delta \cap \operatorname{div}(f)_\infty\).
938	% This is a subset of pure codimension one in \(\Delta\) (AG 9.2), and when we identify
939	% it with a subset of \(V\), it is a subset of \(V\) of codimension one passing through
940	% \(x\) on which \(\varphi\) is not defined."
941	\begin{proof}
942	  
943	  Following Milne's proof of Lemma~3.3. The argument has four sub-steps. Throughout we
944	  fix a representative \((U, \varphi_U)\) of \(f\).
945	
946	  \emph{Sub-step 1: the difference rational map.} Define
947	  \[
948	    \Phi \colon X \times X \dashrightarrow G,
949	    \qquad
950	    \Phi(x, y) \;:=\; \varphi(x) \cdot \varphi(y)^{-1},
951	  \]
952	  via the composition
953	  \[
954	    U \times U
955	    \;\xrightarrow{\varphi_U \times \varphi_U}\; G \times G
956	    \;\xrightarrow{\mathrm{id} \times \mathrm{inv}}\; G \times G
957	    \;\xrightarrow{m}\; G,
958	  \]
959	  where \(\mathrm{inv} \colon G \to G\) is the group-variety inverse and
960	  \(m \colon G \times G \to G\) is the group law. The map \(\Phi\) is rational by
961	  composition: its domain is at least \(U \times U \subseteq X \times X\), which is dense
962	  because \(U\) is dense in \(X\).

965	  \(x\).} If \(\varphi\) is defined at \(x\), the explicit formula \(\Phi(x, x) = e\) (the
966	  identity of \(G\)) shows \(\Phi\) is defined at \((x, x)\). Conversely, if \(\Phi\) is
967	  defined at \((x, x)\), openness of \(\mathrm{Dom}(\Phi)\) in \(X \times X\) produces an
968	  open set \(W \ni (x, x)\) on which \(\Phi\) is defined; by projection (taking the slice
969	  \(\{x\} \times W' \subseteq W\) for some open \(W' \subseteq X\), possibly shrinking
970	  \(W'\) so \(\varphi\) is defined on \(W'\) as well --- \(W'\) need not contain \(x\)), the
971	  identity
972	  \[
973	    \varphi(x) \;=\; \Phi(x, u) \cdot \varphi(u),
974	    \qquad u \in W',
975	  \]
976	  exhibits a regular representative of \(\varphi\) at \(x\). Hence $x \in
977	  \mathrm{Dom}(\varphi)\( if and only if \)(x, x) \in \mathrm{Dom}(\Phi)$.
978	
979	  \emph{Sub-step 3: rephrase via pullback of local rings.} The rational map \(\Phi\)
980	  pulls \(\mathcal O_{G, e}\) back to a \(\bar k\)-subalgebra of the function field
981	  \(K(X \times X)\):
982	  \[
983	    \Phi^\ast \colon \mathcal O_{G, e} \;\longrightarrow\; K(X \times X).
984	  \]
985	  Because \(\Phi\) sends \((x, x)\) to \(e\) wherever it is defined, \(\Phi\) is defined at
986	  \((x, x)\) if and only if $\Phi^\ast(\mathcal O_{G, e}) \subseteq \mathcal O_{X
987	  \times X, (x, x)}\( (i.e.\ every local function on \)G\( near \)e$ has no pole at
988	  \((x, x)\) after pullback).
989	
990	  \emph{Sub-step 4: pole locus is pure codimension \(1\).} The variety \(X \times X\) is
991	  nonsingular (smoothness is stable under products over \(\bar k\)), so the Weil-divisor
992	  apparatus of \cref{def:codim1_cycles} and the order map of
993	  \cref{def:order_at_point} apply on \(X \times X\). For any nonzero rational function
994	  \(f \in K(X \times X)^\ast\) decompose its divisor as
995	  \[
996	    \mathrm{div}(f) \;=\; \mathrm{div}(f)_0 - \mathrm{div}(f)_\infty,
997	  \]
998	  with \(\mathrm{div}(f)_0\) and \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty\) effective and
999	  \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty = \mathrm{div}(f^{-1})_0\) (Milne's notation, matching the
1000	  project's \(\div(f) = \sum \ord_Y(f) \cdot Y\) of
1001	  \cref{def:principal_divisor}: \(\mathrm{div}(f)_0\) collects the prime divisors with
1002	  \(\ord_Y(f) > 0\) and \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty\) those with \(\ord_Y(f) < 0\)). The local
1003	  ring at \((x, x)\) can be characterised as
1004	  \[
1005	    \mathcal O_{X \times X, (x, x)}
1006	    \;=\; \bigl\{\, f \in K(X \times X) \;\big|\;
1007	    \mathrm{div}(f)_\infty \text{ does not contain } (x, x) \,\bigr\} \cup \{0\}.
1008	  \]
1009	  Suppose \(f \in K(X \times X)^\ast\) is not in \(\mathcal O_{X \times X, (x, x)}\);
1010	  equivalently, \((x, x) \in \mathrm{supp}(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty)\). The pole divisor
1011	  \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty\) is a finite sum of prime divisors of \(X \times X\) (i.e.\ a
1012	  closed subset of pure codimension \(1\) in \(X \times X\)). Its intersection with the
1013	  diagonal \(\Delta_X \subseteq X \times X\) is a closed subset of pure codimension \(1\)
1014	  in \(\Delta_X\) (this is Milne's reference to AG~9.2, the standard fact that a prime
1015	  divisor of \(X \times X\) either contains \(\Delta_X\) or meets it in pure codimension
1016	  \(1\); on a nonsingular variety this is an instance of Krull's principal-ideal theorem
1017	  applied to the local equation of \(\Delta_X\) inside \(X \times X\)).
1018	
1019	  \emph{Conclusion.} Suppose \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow G\) is not defined on all of
1020	  \(X\). Pick \(x \in Z(f)\). By Sub-steps~2--3, there is some $f_0 \in
1021	  \Phi^\ast(\mathcal O_{G, e})\( with \)(x, x) \in \mathrm{supp}(\mathrm{div}(f_0)_\infty)$.
1022	  By Sub-step~4 the intersection $\mathrm{supp}(\mathrm{div}(f_0)_\infty) \cap
1023	  \Delta_X\( is pure codimension \)1\( in \)\Delta_X\(, passing through \)(x, x)$.
1024	  Identifying \(\Delta_X\) with \(X\) via the diagonal isomorphism, the corresponding
1025	  closed subset of \(X\) has pure codimension \(1\) and passes through \(x\). By Sub-step~2,
1026	  every point of this codim-\(1\) subset is a point where \(\Phi\) is undefined on the
1027	  diagonal, hence where \(\varphi\) is undefined. Thus \(Z(\varphi)\) contains a pure
1028	  codim-\(1\) closed subset passing through \(x\); since \(x \in Z(\varphi)\) was arbitrary,
1029	  every irreducible component of \(Z(\varphi)\) has codimension exactly \(1\), i.e.\
1030	  \(Z(\varphi)\) is of pure codimension \(1\).
1031	\end{proof}
1032	
1033	\begin{remark}
1034	  \label{rmk:milne_lem33_charfree}
1035	  \textbf{Characteristic-free.} The above proof uses only: (i) the group law on \(G\)
1036	  and its inverse (formal smoothness of \(G\) as a group object); (ii) the Weil-divisor
1037	  apparatus on the nonsingular variety \(X \times X\) (Hartshorne~II.6, or
1038	  \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor} in the project's notation); (iii) the
1039	  decomposition of a divisor on a nonsingular variety into effective pieces, and
1040	  Krull's principal-ideal theorem on \(X \times X\). None of these has a characteristic
1041	  restriction, so \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} holds over every
1042	  algebraically closed base field, in arbitrary characteristic. (Milne states the
1043	  lemma in this generality at \S I.3, p.~17.)
1044	\end{remark}
1045	
1046	\section{Weil-divisor obstruction to codim-1 extension}
1047	\label{sec:weil_obstruction}
1048	
1049	The proof of \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}'s Step~1 (codim-\(1\) ruling-out) goes
1050	through the valuative criterion. In the project's Weil-divisor language
1051	(\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}) the same obstruction has a cleaner phrasing:
1052	the extension fails at a prime divisor \(W\) if and only if some affine coordinate of
1053	\(f\) around the image \(\overline{f(\eta_W)}\) has a strictly negative order
1054	\(\ord_W(\cdot) < 0\). This is the form the Lean prover needs when consuming
1055	\cref{def:order_at_point} and \cref{def:principal_divisor}.
1056	
1057	\begin{theorem}
1058	
1059	  [Weil-divisor characterisation of the codim-1 extension obstruction]
1060	  \label{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction}
1061	  
1062	  % NOTE (iter-179 review, lvb-checker `codimone-iter179` corrective):
1063	  % `\lean{...}` pin DETACHED (Path D2 option (b) per the lvb-checker). The
1064	  % substantive Hartshorne-II.6 valuative reformulation (the iff with
1065	  % ord_W >= 0) below is the *intended* statement; the iter-179 Lane D
1066	  % `mem_domain_iff_exists_partialMap_through_point` decl is only a 2-LOC
1067	  % `Scheme.RationalMap.mem_domain` reshuffle that does NOT prove the
1068	  % ord_W content, so re-pinning `\lean{...}` to it would falsely claim
1069	  % kernel-clean formalization of the substantive Hartshorne iff. The
1070	  % weaker reshuffle proven this iter will be pinned to its own light
1071	  % `\begin{lemma}` block (working title `lem:mem_domain_partial_map_reshuffle`)
1072	  % by an iter-180+ blueprint-writer dispatch; the substantive Hartshorne
1073	  % version stays pinned here but unlinked until a future
1074	  % `extend_iff_pullback_order_nonneg` lemma carries
1075	  % `Scheme.RationalMap.order` machinery (pullback `RationalMap →
1076	  % function-field ring map`, not yet in Mathlib at b80f227). See
1077	  % task_results/lean-vs-blueprint-checker-codimone-iter179.md +
1078	  % proof-journal/sessions/session_179/recommendations.md REC-14.
1079	  % NOTE (iter-194 writer): no corresponding Lean declaration found in
1080	  % CodimOneExtension.lean --- iter-195 must either rename to match an
1081	  % existing decl or add a new Lean stub. (The iter-179 detach was
1082	  % structural, not a \lean{...} typo: the substantive Hartshorne-II.6
1083	  % ord_W >= 0 reformulation requires the
1084	  % Scheme.RationalMap-to-function-field pullback machinery, which is
1085	  % not in Mathlib at commit b80f227 and is not yet built in this
1086	  % project. The weaker iter-179 reshuffle Lean declaration
1087	  % mem_domain_iff_exists_partialMap_through_point is blueprint-pinned
1088	  % at \cref{lem:mem_domain_partial_map_reshuffle} as the honest
1089	  % fallback; thm:weil_divisor_obstruction here remains unpinned at the
1090	  % \lean{...} level until the genuine pullback decl lands.)
1091	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, pp.~130--131 (the valuation \(v_Y\) associated to a
1092	  % prime divisor, and the characterisation of \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta_Y}\) as the
1093	  % subring of \(K(X)\) on which \(v_Y \geq 0\)) (read from
1094	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 147--148).
1095	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "If \(Y\) is a prime divisor on \(X\), let \(\eta \in Y\) be its generic
1096	  % point. Then the local ring \(\mathcal O_{\eta, X}\) of \(X\) at \(\eta\) is a discrete
1097	  % valuation ring with quotient field \(K\), the function field of \(X\). We call the
1098	  % corresponding discrete valuation \(v_Y\) the \emph{valuation of \(Y\)}."
1099	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now let \(f \in K^\ast\) be any nonzero rational function on \(X\).
1100	  % Then \(v_Y(f)\) is an integer. If it is positive, we say \(f\) has a \emph{zero} along
1101	  % \(Y\), of that order; if it is negative, we say \(f\) has a \emph{pole} along \(Y\), of
1102	  % order \(-v_Y(f)\)."
1103	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, pp.~130--131 (valuation \(v_Y\) and the order map).}
1104	  Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over \(\bar k\), let \(Y\) be a variety over \(\bar k\),
1105	  let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map, and let \(W \subseteq X\) be a
1106	  prime divisor with generic point \(\eta_W \in W\) (so
1107	  \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{\eta_W\}} = 1\)). Let \((U, \varphi_U)\) be any representative of
1108	  \(f\) with \(U \cap (X \smallsetminus W) \neq \emptyset\). The following are equivalent:
1109	  \begin{enumerate}
1110	    \item \(f\) is defined at \(\eta_W\) (i.e.\ \(\eta_W \in \mathrm{Dom}(f)\), equivalently
1111	      \(W \not\subseteq Z(f)\)).
1112	    \item For some (equivalently, every) affine open neighbourhood \(V \subseteq Y\) of
1113	      the generic image of \(f\) near \(W\), every regular function $g \in
1114	      \mathcal O_Y(V)\( pulls back to a rational function \)\varphi_U^\ast(g) \in K(X)$
1115	      satisfying
1116	      \[
1117	        \ord_W\bigl(\varphi_U^\ast(g)\bigr) \;\geq\; 0.
1118	      \]
1119	  \end{enumerate}
1120	
1121	  Consequently: \(f\) is codim-\(1\)-indeterminacy-free
1122	  (\cref{def:codim_one_indeterminacy}) if and only if, for every prime divisor
1123	  \(W \subseteq X\) and every affine open \(V \subseteq Y\) as above, the pullback
1124	  \(\varphi_U^\ast(g)\) has \(\ord_W \geq 0\) for all \(g \in \mathcal O_Y(V)\).
1125	\end{theorem}
1126	
1127	\begin{proof}
1128	  
1129	  By \cref{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr}, the local ring \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta_W}\) is a
1130	  DVR with fraction field \(K(X)\) and uniformiser determined by \(W\); by
1131	  \cref{def:order_at_point} its valuation is exactly \(\ord_W\). The local ring at
1132	  \(\eta_W\) is therefore characterised inside \(K(X)\) as the subring
1133	  \[
1134	    \mathcal O_{X, \eta_W} \;=\; \bigl\{\, h \in K(X) \;\big|\; \ord_W(h) \geq 0
1135	    \,\bigr\} \cup \{0\}.
1136	  \]
1137	  The rational map \(f\) is defined at \(\eta_W\) if and only if, for any affine open
1138	  \(V = \operatorname{Spec} A \subseteq Y\) around the image \(\overline{f(\eta_W)}\),
1139	  the pullback \(\varphi_U^\ast \colon A \to K(X)\) lands in
1140	  \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta_W}\): this is the standard local characterisation of where a
1141	  rational map is regular at a codim-\(1\) point of a smooth variety (it is the
1142	  ``regular = no pole'' criterion). By the displayed identity, the latter is
1143	  equivalent to \(\ord_W(\varphi_U^\ast(g)) \geq 0\) for every \(g \in A\), which is
1144	  condition (2). The equivalence (1)~\(\Leftrightarrow\)~(2) is therefore established.
1145	
1146	  For the consequence, \(f\) is codim-\(1\)-indeterminacy-free iff no prime divisor \(W\) is
1147	  contained in \(Z(f)\), which by the equivalence above is iff the \(\ord_W \geq 0\)
1148	  condition holds for every prime divisor \(W\).
1149	\end{proof}
1150	
1151	\begin{remark}
1152	  \label{rmk:weil_obstruction_application}
1153	  \textbf{Why this form is the prover-facing one.} In the consumer chapter
1154	  \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension} the Lean prover combines
1155	  \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} with \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy}; in
1156	  isolation, the abstract codim-\(\geq 2\) statement
1157	  \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} suffices and \cref{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction}
1158	  is not invoked. The Weil-divisor form is the bridge to the project's
1159	  \(\mathrm{Scheme.WeilDivisor}\) API, which is the form A.4.a's own internal sub-proofs
1160	  use when establishing \emph{specific} extensions in genus-\(0\) classifying-map
1161	  arguments and in the symmetric-product Albanese chapter
1162	  \cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}.
1163	\end{remark}

1165	\begin{lemma}
1166	
1167	\leanok
1168	  [Definedness at a prime divisor, repackaged as a through-the-point partial map]
1169	  \label{lem:mem_domain_partial_map_reshuffle}
1170	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.mem_domain_iff_exists_partialMap_through_point}
1171	  Let \(X\) and \(Y\) be varieties over \(\bar k\) and let
1172	  \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map. For a prime divisor
1173	  \(W \subseteq X\) with generic point \(\eta_W = W.\mathrm{point}\), the
1174	  following are equivalent:
1175	  \begin{enumerate}
1176	    \item \(\eta_W \in \mathrm{Dom}(f)\) (\(f\) is defined at the generic point of
1177	      \(W\)).
1178	    \item There exists a partial-map representative \((U, \varphi_U)\) of \(f\) ---
1179	      i.e.\ a regular morphism \(\varphi_U \colon U \to Y\) on a dense open
1180	      \(U \subseteq X\) whose induced rational map equals \(f\) --- whose domain
1181	      \(U\) contains \(\eta_W\).
1182	  \end{enumerate}
1183	\end{lemma}
1184	
1185	\begin{proof}
1186	
1187	  The lemma is a definitional reshuffle of the Mathlib membership predicate
1188	  \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.mem\_domain}, which already states that
1189	  \(\eta_W \in \mathrm{Dom}(f)\) iff there is some partial-map representative of
1190	  \(f\) defined at \(\eta_W\); the only content here is reordering the two
1191	  conjuncts under the existential so the \(\varphi_U \mapsto f\) component lands
1192	  first (matching the way later witnesses in the project construct partial-map
1193	  representatives). The forward and backward implications are immediate from
1194	  the unfolded definition.
1195	\end{proof}
1196	
1197	\begin{remark}
1198	  \label{rmk:mem_domain_partial_map_reshuffle_scope}

1200	  \cref{lem:mem_domain_partial_map_reshuffle} is the iter-179 Lane~D honest
1201	  fallback for the substantive Hartshorne-II.6 ``regular = no pole''
1202	  reformulation that \cref{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction} states in its full
1203	  \(\ord_W \geq 0\) form. The reshuffle captures only the
1204	  definitional half of the equivalence (existence of a partial-map
1205	  representative through a prime-divisor generic point); upgrading it to the

1208	  not shipped by Mathlib at commit \texttt{b80f227}. See the iter-179
1209	  \texttt{\% NOTE} block on \cref{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction} for the

1308	    lemma using the \texttt{Module.depth} / Auslander--Buchsbaum machinery of
1309	    \cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum}, or (ii) reformulate
1310	    \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} in the weaker ``surface'' form
1311	    (where \(X\) has \(\dim 2\), so codim-\(\geq 2\) means closed points only and the
1312	    extension is the classical Hartshorne~III.7.1 ``smooth-surface point removal''
1313	    argument). For the consumer \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension} only
1314	    the surface case is needed in practice (the symmetric product \(C^{(g)}\) is
1315	    smooth, and the Abel--Jacobi map's intermediate-step indeterminacy lives on a
1316	    smooth surface inside \(C^{(g)} \times C^{(g)}\)).
1317	\end{itemize}
1318	
1319	No new core axiom is required. The proof of \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} is
1320	purely Weil-divisor manipulation on a smooth variety and is the cleanest part of
1321	the chapter; the substance of the Lean engineering is in
1322	\cref{thm:codim_one_extension}'s proof, both halves of which rest on Mathlib API
1323	gaps the prover must either fill in or work around.
1324	
1325	\section{Out of scope}
1326	\label{sec:codim1_out_of_scope}
1327	
1328	This chapter packages \emph{only} the two extension inputs and the Weil-divisor
1329	reformulation needed for A.4.a. The following adjacent content is explicitly out of
1330	scope:
1331	\begin{itemize}
1332	  \item \textbf{The Auslander--Buchsbaum formula and the regular \(\Rightarrow\)
1333	    Cohen--Macaulay corollary.} Proved in \cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum}
1334	    as \cref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} and \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}; this
1335	    chapter only consumes them (in Step~2 of \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}; see
1336	    \cref{rmk:codim1_role_of_ab}).
1337	  \item \textbf{Milne Theorem~3.2 itself.} The combination of
1338	    \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} and \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} into the
1339	    statement ``a rational map from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety
1340	    extends'' lives in \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension} as
1341	    \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}; this chapter only supplies the two inputs.
1342	  \item \textbf{Milne Theorem~3.4 / Corollary~3.7 / Theorem~3.8 / Proposition~3.9 /
1343	    Proposition~3.10.} The remainder of Milne~\S I.3 (the ``map between AVs is
1344	    translated homomorphism'' family of results, and the
1345	    \(\mathbb A^1 \dashrightarrow A\) / unirational\(\dashrightarrow A\) constancy
1346	    statements) is downstream of \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} but is not
1347	    needed by A.4 in the project's current routing: the genus-\(0\) base case is
1348	    handled via the proven Rigidity Lemma + \(\mathbb G_m\)-scaling shortcut
1349	    (\cref{chap:RigidityKbar}), and the positive-genus arm consumes only the bare
1350	    Theorem~3.2 statement.
1351	  \item \textbf{Weil-divisor degree on \(X \times X\) for \(X\) a curve.} The proof of
1352	    \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} invokes the Weil-divisor apparatus on the
1353	    smooth surface \(X \times X\) but does \emph{not} need the degree map of
1354	    \cref{def:divisor_degree} (which is curve-specific); only the codim-\(1\)
1355	    decomposition \(\div(f) = \div(f)_0 - \div(f)_\infty\) on a nonsingular surface is
1356	    used. The bilinear degree formula on \(X \times X\) for \(X\) a curve (intersection
1357	    numbers, Hartshorne~V.1) is out of scope.
1358	  \item \textbf{Cartier divisors and the Cartier/Weil comparison.} On a smooth variety
1359	    they coincide and the chapter uses Weil throughout; the Cartier formalism is not
1360	    introduced.
1361	  \item \textbf{Generalisation to non-algebraically-closed base fields.} The
1362	    project's positive-genus arm runs over \(\bar k\) throughout; descent of the
1363	    extension to a non-algebraically-closed base, if ever needed, would require
1364	    Galois-descent machinery and is not in scope.
1365	  \item \textbf{Resolution of indeterminacy by blow-ups} (Hironaka-style, for higher
1366	    indeterminacy codimensions on a smooth variety with a non-group-variety target):
1367	    out of scope. The combination of \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} and
1368	    \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} for \(G\) a group variety is sharper than any
1369	    blow-up-based approach because it produces an extension on the original \(X\)
1370	    rather than on a blow-up; no blow-up apparatus is needed.
1371	\end{itemize}

# Analogy: Stacks 00OE — Krull-dim of smooth k-algebra at a maximal ideal

## Mode
cross-domain-inspiration

## Slug
coe-stacks00oe

## Iteration
200

## Structural problem (abstracted)

Given a smooth `R`-algebra `S` of relative dimension `n` and a maximal
ideal `m ⊂ S`, prove `ringKrullDim (S_m) = n` (equivalently
`m.height = n`). The structural shape is: "Krull dim of a localization
at a closed point equals a numerical invariant that comes from a
*finite presentation* of `S` over the base ring `R`". The numerical
invariant is `#generators − #relations` for a standard-smooth
presentation; the closed-point dimension is computed by combining
(a) the *Krull dim of the polynomial-ring localization at a maximal*
(known closed form: `=#generators`) with (b) "cut by a regular
sequence of length `#relations`" (drops dim by exactly the length).

## Failed approaches (from directive)

- **Stacks 00OE direct (fiber-dim + going-down)**: structurally sound
  but invokes an unbroken chain of Mathlib lemmas in sequence
  (going-down, fiber dim, polynomial-ring dim, Noether normalization
  in some variants) that requires careful chaining.
- **iter-198/199 Lane COE exploration**: no closure attempt committed;
  no specific failed Lean route to avoid recommending.

The failed-approach list is empty in the "what NOT to port back" sense
— the project hasn't tried and rejected anything. Every Mathlib
analogue below is fair game.

## Analogues found

Ranked by porting cost (lowest first). Mathlib does NOT package
Stacks 00OE as a single named lemma; it ships every *building block*
in usable form, so the project's task is **chaining**, not building
new infrastructure.

### Analogue 1: `Polynomial.height_eq_height_add_one`

- **Domain**: commutative algebra / Krull-dimension theory of
  polynomial rings. `Mathlib.RingTheory.KrullDimension.Polynomial`.
- **Same structural problem there**: for `R` Noetherian and any
  maximal ideal `P ⊂ R[X]` lying over a (necessarily prime) ideal
  `p ⊂ R`, we have `P.height = p.height + 1`. This is exactly the
  inductive step underlying "every maximal ideal in `k[x_1,…,x_n]`
  has height `n`" — iterate `n` times starting from a field
  (`p = 0`, `p.height = 0`).
- **Technique**: the proof goes by reducing to the case where `p`
  itself is maximal (using `Polynomial.isMaximal_comap_C_of_isJacobsonRing`
  / general height-bound machinery), then invoking Krull's principal
  ideal theorem to bound `P.height ≤ p.height + 1` and equality via
  an explicit chain of primes `(0) ⊂ pR[X] ⊂ P`.
- **Mapping to project**: for the closed-fiber part of Stacks 00OE,
  pull back `m ⊂ S` along the standard-smooth presentation surjection
  `R[ι] → S` to get a maximal ideal `m' = preimage(m) ∪ {f_j}` in
  `R[ι]`. The "polynomial-ring height" half computes
  `(preimage of m in R[ι])_localized.height = #ι` by iterating this
  lemma `#ι` times over the base ring `R` (here a field, so
  `R.ringKrullDim = 0` via `ringKrullDim_eq_zero_of_field`).
- **Porting cost**: low (≤ 30 LOC for the polynomial-ring induction
  packaged as a project-side helper). No new Mathlib gap.
- **Verdict**: ANALOGUE_FOUND.

### Analogue 2: `ringKrullDim_add_length_eq_ringKrullDim_of_isRegular`

- **Domain**: commutative algebra / regular-sequence theory.
  `Mathlib.RingTheory.KrullDimension.Regular`.
- **Same structural problem there**: for `R` Noetherian local and a
  regular sequence `rs : List R`,
  `ringKrullDim (R ⧸ Ideal.ofList rs) + rs.length = ringKrullDim R`.
  This is the *exact* statement Stacks 00OE invokes to drop dim by
  the number of relations: "modding out by a regular sequence
  decreases Krull dimension by its length".
- **Technique**: induction on the sequence length combined with
  `ringKrullDim_quotient_span_singleton_succ_eq_ringKrullDim` (the
  single-element case, which is Krull's principal ideal theorem in
  the form "a regular element drops dim by 1"). The single-step
  proof uses `IsSMulRegular` + non-zero-divisor membership of the
  maximal ideal.
- **Mapping to project**: for the standard-smooth presentation
  `S = R[ι] ⧸ (f_j, j ∈ σ)` with Jacobian matrix invertible, localize
  at `m' = preimage(m) ⊂ R[ι]`. Apply this lemma to
  `R' := R[ι]_{m'}` with the regular sequence `(f_j)_{j ∈ σ}`:
  `ringKrullDim (R' ⧸ (f_j)) + #σ = ringKrullDim R'`. The LHS is
  `ringKrullDim S_m` (since localization commutes with quotient
  by an ideal in the prime), the RHS is `#ι` by Analogue 1, and
  `n = #ι − #σ`.
- **Residual obligation**: show `(f_j)_{j ∈ σ}` is a regular sequence
  in `R[ι]_{m'}`. This is genuine content — it's the algebraic
  meaning of "the Jacobian is invertible" (Stacks tag 00SW / 00OW
  for the smooth case). In Mathlib at commit `b80f227` this
  regular-sequence-from-Jacobian step is not packaged; it must be
  built project-side as a 30–60 LOC helper that consumes
  `Algebra.SubmersivePresentation.jacobian_isUnit` and concludes
  `IsWeaklyRegular` via `IsLocalRing.isRegular_iff_isWeaklyRegular_of_subset_maximalIdeal`.
- **Porting cost**: medium. The dim-drop lemma is a direct apply;
  the regular-sequence construction from the Jacobian is the
  substantive new helper (~30–60 LOC, axiom-clean against
  Mathlib's SubmersivePresentation API).
- **Verdict**: ANALOGUE_FOUND. **Top recommendation for the
  dim-arithmetic backbone.**

### Analogue 3: `Ideal.height_eq_height_add_of_liesOver_of_hasGoingDown`

- **Domain**: commutative algebra / going-down for ring extensions.
  `Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.KrullsHeightTheorem`.
- **Same structural problem there**: for `R`, `S` Noetherian with
  `R → S` having going-down (e.g. flat), `p ⊂ R` prime, `P ⊂ S`
  prime over `p`:
  `P.height = p.height + (height of P/pS in S/pS)`.
  This is the *fiber-dimension formula* — height of a prime in `S`
  decomposes as height in the base plus height in the closed fiber.
- **Technique**: combines `Algebra.HasGoingDown.iff_generalizingMap_primeSpectrumComap`
  (going-down ⟺ generalization-preserving on Spec) with a chain-of-primes
  argument that lifts a maximal chain in `S/pS` and a maximal chain in
  `R` below `p` to a single maximal chain in `S` through `P`.
- **Mapping to project**: with `R = k` (field), `S` smooth over `k`,
  this fires with `p = 0`, `p.height = 0`, going-down free via
  `Algebra.Smooth.flat` ⟹ `Algebra.HasGoingDown.of_flat`. The formula
  reduces to `m.height = (height of m/0·S in S/0·S) = m.height in S`
  — i.e. a TAUTOLOGY when the base is a field. So this analogue
  shines NOT for the smooth-over-field case directly, but as a
  *re-usable middle step* when one wants to compose the smooth case
  with the Noether-normalization reduction (where the polynomial
  subring is itself an intermediate `R'` with non-trivial height).
- **Porting cost**: low if used as a re-export. But not the right
  driver for the field-base case.
- **Verdict**: PARTIAL_ANALOGUE — applicable but not the central
  technique for the specific closure the project needs (`R = k̄`).

### Analogue 4: `IsLocalization.AtPrime.ringKrullDim_eq_height` + `IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal_height_eq_ringKrullDim`

- **Domain**: commutative algebra / localization-and-dimension API.
  `Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.Height`.
- **Same structural problem there**: bridging `ringKrullDim` of a
  localization with `Ideal.height` of the localized prime:
  `ringKrullDim (Localization.atPrime p) = ↑p.height`, and the
  dual `↑(maximalIdeal R).height = ringKrullDim R` for any local R.
- **Technique**: definitional unfolding of `ringKrullDim` as the
  sup of `Ideal.primeHeight` over `IsPrime`, combined with the
  bijection `Spec(R_p) ↔ {q ∈ Spec R | q ≤ p}` that swaps the sup
  for `p.height`.
- **Mapping to project**: this is the *boundary translator* — the
  project's `isRegularLocalRing_stalk_of_smooth` goal lives on
  `ringKrullDim (X.left.presheaf.stalk z)`, which is
  `ringKrullDim (Localization.atPrime m)` via the project's
  `Scheme.ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight` bridge (iter-183). To
  consume Analogues 1+2 (which speak `Ideal.height`), the proof
  starts with `IsLocalization.AtPrime.ringKrullDim_eq_height` to
  recast the goal as `m.height = n`, then runs the
  height-arithmetic chain.
- **Porting cost**: trivial (one rewrite).
- **Verdict**: ANALOGUE_FOUND. **Required adapter; not a stand-alone
  technique.**

### Analogue 5: `MvPolynomial.ringKrullDim_of_isNoetherianRing`

- **Domain**: commutative algebra / polynomial-ring dimension.
  `Mathlib.RingTheory.KrullDimension.Polynomial`.
- **Same structural problem there**: for `R` Noetherian and `ι` finite,
  `ringKrullDim (MvPolynomial ι R) = ringKrullDim R + ↑(Nat.card ι)`.
  Combined with `ringKrullDim_eq_zero_of_field`, this gives
  `ringKrullDim (k[ι]) = #ι` for the polynomial ring over a field.
- **Technique**: induction on `#ι`, reducing to `Polynomial.ringKrullDim_of_isNoetherianRing`,
  which in turn invokes a chain argument: `0 ⊂ X·R[X] ⊂ M` for any
  maximal `M`, plus a height-upper-bound from Krull's principal
  ideal theorem.
- **Mapping to project**: useful if the project wants to bound
  `m'.height ≤ ringKrullDim k[ι] = #ι` rather than computing it
  directly via Analogue 1. For the LOWER bound (the substantive
  half) the project still needs Analogue 1's per-maximal-ideal
  iteration. So this gives a one-line upper bound but not the
  equality.
- **Porting cost**: trivial as upper-bound; equality needs Analogue 1.
- **Verdict**: PARTIAL_ANALOGUE — bound-only, not equality.

### Analogue 6: `Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.rank_kaehlerDifferential`

- **Domain**: commutative algebra / Kähler differentials of
  standard-smooth algebras. `Mathlib.RingTheory.Smooth.StandardSmoothCotangent`.
- **Same structural problem there**: connects the `relativeDimension`
  numerical invariant with the rank of `Ω[S⁄R]` as an `S`-module:
  `Module.rank S Ω[S⁄R] = ↑n` for standard-smooth of rel dim `n`.
  This is the project's *iter-198 (i)-resolution* path — extract a
  specific `n` from the smooth structure and then use it consistently.
- **Technique**: extracts a `SubmersivePresentation` from the
  `IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension` instance and uses the
  basis-of-Kähler-differentials computation for the presentation.
- **Mapping to project**: this is what the iter-198 closure of
  sub-gap (i) already rides on (the project's `rank_kaehlerDifferential_localization_eq_relativeDimension`).
  It does NOT directly give Krull-dim — Mathlib has no lemma
  `Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.ringKrullDim_eq` or
  `_at_maximal_height_eq` to short-circuit Stacks 00OE.

`_at_maximal_height_eq` to short-circuit Stacks 00OE.
- **Porting cost**: zero (already in use in the project at
  iter-198).
- **Verdict**: NO_USEFUL_ANALOGUE for sub-gap (ii.B) directly — it
  is the *cotangent-side* invariant computation, distinct from the
  Krull-dim side that (ii.B) needs.

## Top suggestion

**Compose Analogues 4 + 1 + 2** as a project-side helper chain
`smoothAlgebra_relativeDim_ringKrullDim_localization`. The recipe in
three steps:

```text
Setup (existing iter-198/199 helpers do this):
  S = standard-smooth k-algebra of relative dim n
  m ⊂ S maximal
  S_m = Localization.AtPrime S m (already wired via Stage 3)
  P : SubmersivePresentation k S ι σ with P.dimension = n   -- iter-198
  R'  := MvPolynomial ι k                                   -- the presentation source
  m'  := preimage of m in R'                                -- maximal in R'
  R'_{m'} := Localization.AtPrime R' m'
  Surjection ϕ : R'_{m'} ↠ S_m with kernel = (f_j)_{j ∈ σ}  -- localized presentation

Step 1 — recast Krull-dim as height:
  rw [IsLocalization.AtPrime.ringKrullDim_eq_height m S_m]
  -- Goal:  m.height = ↑n

Step 2 — compute the polynomial-ring height:
  have hPoly : m'.height = ↑(Nat.card ι) := by
    -- iterate Polynomial.height_eq_height_add_one #ι times,
    -- bottoming out at p = 0 with p.height = 0 (k is a field)
    sorry  -- ≤ 30 LOC packaged helper

Step 3 — drop by the regular-sequence length:
  -- Convert Stage 1 (smooth ⟹ flat) + iter-198 SubmersivePresentation
  -- + Jacobian_isUnit into:
  have hReg : RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular R'_{m'} (relations ϕ)
  have hLen : (relations ϕ).length = Nat.card σ
  have hDimRel :
    ringKrullDim (R'_{m'} ⧸ Ideal.ofList (relations ϕ)) + Nat.card σ
      = ringKrullDim R'_{m'} :=
    ringKrullDim_add_length_eq_ringKrullDim_of_isRegular _ hReg
  -- LHS ⧸ side = ringKrullDim S_m via ϕ
  -- RHS = #ι via Step 2 + IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal_height_eq_ringKrullDim
  -- Solve for ringKrullDim S_m = #ι − #σ = n
  arithmetic
```

**The substantive residual obligation is Step 3's `hReg`** —
showing the relations of a SubmersivePresentation form a regular
sequence in `R'_{m'}`. This is Stacks 00SW (in the smooth-over-flat
case) / 00OW (Jacobian criterion); the abstract content is "the
Jacobian matrix being invertible at the closed point makes the
relations Koszul-regular". In Mathlib's current state at
`b80f227`, this is the only piece **not** ready to apply directly.

A reasonable project-side build estimate for the residual:
30–60 LOC, axiom-clean against
`Algebra.SubmersivePresentation.jacobian_isUnit` +
`RingTheory.Sequence.isRegular_cons_iff` +
`IsLocalRing.isRegular_iff_isWeaklyRegular_of_subset_maximalIdeal`.

Steps 1 + 2 + the surjection-arithmetic in Step 3 total ≈ 50 LOC of
pure chaining of existing Mathlib lemmas.

`Albanese/CodimOneExtension.lean` (or split into a dedicated
`Picard/SmoothAlgebraKrullDim.lean` helper). This is **below** the
directive's "200–300 LOC on top of `transcendenceDegree` +
Noether-normalization" estimate, because the route via the
SubmersivePresentation already in hand (from iter-198) bypasses
the explicit Noether normalization step.

`AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/CodimOneExtension.lean` around line 836
(the comment block flagged "Sub-gap (ii.B)"), or a new

single helper `ringKrullDim_localization_of_isStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension`
which the main theorem consumes via one rewrite.

## Fallback route

If Step 3's `hReg` construction proves harder than 60 LOC (the
SubmersivePresentation's relations are in `MvPolynomial ι R`, not
in `R'_{m'}`, so a base-change/extension-of-scalars argument is
needed to lift them as regular in the localization), fall back to

`ringKrullDim_le_ringKrullDim_add_spanFinrank` to get
`ringKrullDim S_m ≤ ringKrullDim (S_m/m) + #generators of m`. With
`S_m/m = κ` (dim 0) and `#generators of m = finrank κ (m/m²) = n`
(by iter-199's (ii.A) closure), this gives **the upper bound
`ringKrullDim S_m ≤ n`**. For the lower bound, descend along the
flat map `k → S_m` and use Krull's height theorem in the form
`Ideal.height_le_spanFinrank` reversed via the Jacobian regularity.

finrank already in hand.

- **`Algebra.trdeg` (transcendence-degree route)**: Mathlib has

- **Direct `IsRegularLocalRing.iff_finrank_cotangentSpace` consumer

{"file_path":"/home/archon/.elan/toolchains/leanprover--lean4---v4.30.0-rc2/src/lean/Lean/Elab/Declaration.lean","content":"/-
